content.problemStatement: Robot Bunny is lost. It wants to reach its home as soon as possible. Currently it is standing at coordinates **(x1, y1)** in 2-D plane. Its home is at coordinates **(x2, y2)**. Bunny is extremely worried. Please help it by giving a command by telling the direction in which it should to go so as to reach its home. If you give it a direction, it will keep moving in that direction till it reaches its home. There are four possible directions you can give as command - **"left", "right", "up", "down"**. It might be possible that you can't instruct the robot in such a way that it reaches its home. In that case, output **"sad"**.
constraints: The initial position of the robot is not its home
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases. Each test case consists of a single line containing four space-separated integers x1, y1, x2, y2.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the direction in which the robot should move to reach its home, or 'sad' if it's impossible.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case, the robot moves right to reach its home. In the second test case, the robot moves up to reach its home. In the third test case, it's impossible for the robot to reach its home by moving in a single direction.
In the first test case, the robot moves up to reach its home. In the second test case, the robot moves down to reach its home.
The robot is already at its home, but the problem statement guarantees that the initial position of the robot is not its home. Therefore, this test case is invalid.

solution.approach: To determine the direction in which the robot should move, we compare the x and y coordinates of the robot's current position and its home. If the x coordinates are different, the robot should move left or right. If the y coordinates are different, the robot should move up or down. If both x and y coordinates are different, it's impossible for the robot to reach its home by moving in a single direction.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) — the algorithm processes each test case in constant time, so the total time complexity is linear with respect to the number of test cases
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the algorithm uses a constant amount of space to store the input and output
